course_description: Economics focuses on choices between alternative ways of using resources and the consequences of these choices, which often have to be made in the face of problems associated with the availability of information and limitations to knowledge. These choices concern issues such as what to consume, what to produce, how to produce it, how to organize production between different economic entities, and what prices to charge. Choices taken by individuals need to be coordinated to avoid wasted opportunities, the more so the more that specialization is taking place at the level of the individual, firm or national economy. A major coordinating role is played by price mechanisms, i.e. adjustments in relative prices and the incentives provided by profits and losses. However, much coordination is achieved via the use of planning and institutions. Even with coordination assisted by these devices coordination failures can still occur, such as major slumps in employment and international trade. Economic theory can help to assess when these problems are likely and how they may be dealt with.

requirements.incompatible: ECON2010, or 2400 or 7003 or 7010 or 7011 or 7110 or MEBS7103
requirements.assumed_background: No background in economics is assumed. However, if you want to do some reading before the course starts so as to get a feel for the economic way of thinking, then a good source for microeconomics would be Tim Hardford (2006) The Undercover Economist , Oxford, Oxford University Press. For macroeconomics, perhaps try Michael Stewart, Keynes and After (various editions), Harmondsworth, Penguin. Reading the articles in The Economist is also a good way of gaining insight into some contempory economic issues.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: This course aims to enable you to develop the ability to link economic theory with everyday issues so as to encourage critical, independent thinking to promote alternative views and new solutions.

learning_outcomes.description: Identify situations where economic principles and theories are applicable to and directly assist in business or organisational decision making.
Acquire a recognition of the resource allocation problem and how economic theory assists in making rational decisions.
Apply economic theory to business problems in an informed and analytical way.
Critically evaluate choices made by individuals and firms using economic concepts.

assessment_details.title: Individual Essay
assessment_details.task_description:
Major Assignment.

The focus of this course is to better understand economic concepts so as to then apply them in business situations. This, in part, will require interventions at times by managers and choices to be made in order to coordinate information and knowledge to avoid wasted opportunities. The course also highlights the importance of understanding the roles consumption, investment, and government actions play in taking business decisions, coordinating activities, and assessing strategic directions.

Economic theories and concepts used in this course have examined how and why businesses and markets work the way they do. With this knowledge, students have been given a tool to assist in making more informed, strategic, business decisions.

Using the perspectives outlined above, write an essay reflecting on a variety of economic concepts that impact your organisation (where you are currently working, or have worked in the past). The essay must address the following points.

An Executive Summary is to be provided (note: these words ARE to be included in the assignment word count). Details of what is needed in an Executive Summary can be found on Blackboard under “Resources”.

Background information on the student’s current (or previous) workplace firm or organisation is to be briefly outlined. The integration of relevant economic terminology should be used to clearly set the scene for the following sections.

Using economic theory (incorporating graphs and relevant calculations), critically analyse past performances of your firm in relation to the market it operated in. Similarly, critically analyse the current performance of your firm in relation to the current market environment it now operates in.

Based on the critical analysis, a detailed reflection is provided on what was learnt, what was confirmed, and what remains puzzling about your organisation and the market it operates in. Clear linkages should be evident from your analysis and reflection to reveal what you perceive as the key strategic issue your firm faces either now or in the future. It is advisable to use additional relevant analytical tools such as SWOT, PESTEL, Porter’s Five Force analysis etc. to provide insights to the economic analysis of your firm’s past, present and future performances. This should also help you identify the key strategic issue your firm faces. Any analysis using SWOT etc. needs to be included in an Appendix and NOT in the body of the assignment. Clearly any findings from such analysis should be used in the body of the assignment.

Note: the words in the Appendix are NOT to be included in assignment word count.

Based on the lesson(s) you learnt from your critical analysis, and combining this with your identified key strategic issue, you must list two clear options to address the key strategic issue. Both options need to be able to be implemented. For each option, use economic theory to fully evaluate how each option addresses the strategic issue and highlight the benefits to the firm of implementing each option. Be sure to discuss and assess the assumptions you will have made when evaluating each option, and look at the impacts on your firm for each option if your assumptions prove to be incorrect.

Draws a conclusion from your analysis. Provides a clearly supported recommendation(s) for the management to address the identified strategic issue, along with implementation guidelines.

Self-assess your assignment using the Major Assignment Marking Criteria word document that is available on Blackboard. Please ensure you honestly self-assess. This self-assessment will not influence the grade awarded for this piece of assessment. However, your self-assessment thoughts are intended to be incorporated into the marker’s feedback. Please ensure your completed self-assessment Major Assignment Marking Criteria sheet is attached as the last page of your assignment.

Submit your assignment online via Blackboard by the due date. To do this, go to the “Assessment” tab on Blackboard and follow the instructions there. Please note that you have to submit the assignment through both Blackboard AND Turnitin by the due date.

NOTE: The essay is not to exceed 2,500 words which includes the Executive Summary. Please note that words exceeding the 2,500 limit will not be marked. Appropriate headings should be used in the essay that guides the reader.

When submitting your assignment, please check: the first page is a cover page (with your name and student number), followed by your written assignment, then the marking criteria sheet with your self-assessment and mark.

assessment_details.title: Tutorial Case Analysis
assessment_details.task_description:
Group Presentation.

Students are required to form small groups of four to five students and this will depend on final enrolment numbers. The presentation by the group is to be 15 minutes, followed by 10 minutes discussion, then class feedback. The topic, date and time for each presentation will be allocated in the first tutorial after the first lecture. Note : final group sizes and length of presentation will depend on actual final enrolment numbers. Details indicated here may vary and will be finalised during the first tutorial.

Each group will be allocated an ASX listed company nominated by the course coordinator. Students are expected to access information from company websites, the ASX website, news articles, or other similarly available sources. Students should not contact or approach any company directly for information. The specific requirements of the group presentation are summarised in the “Group Presentation Marking Guide” (see attached link below, as well as posted on Blackboard). Students are expected to fully address each of the specific requirements of the Group Presentation, which are:

Using economic theory, analyse and explain past company performances . A brief overview of the company will be needed to set the scene. You must assess whether or not previous management strategic choices can be justified using economic theory. Based on your historical analysis, clearly identify and outline the lessons your group believes the company should learn, or has learnt, that would be beneficial to the company.

Having considered the past, examine the present and future direction of the company. Conduct an internal and external environmental analysis of the company using analytical tools of your choice (e.g., SWOT, PESTEL, Porter's Five Forces, etc.). The details of any analysis are NOT to be included in the presentation. However, the group is required to very briefly summarise the main findings of your environmental analysis to paint a clear picture of the environment the company currently operates in. This should result in your group being able to justify, and clearly state, the key strategic issue the company currently faces.

Based on past performances, the lessons learnt, current environment and the company’s position, propose one option to overcome your identified key strategic issue . Fully justify why this option will work using economic theory. You need to also show how it can be implemented by the company (time lines, available resources, etc.) and that it will work in practice.

After your presentation, the group must then have at least one question prepared to promote class discussion and/or debate. The group (or nominated member) is required to lead this class discussion for 10 minutes. The discussion should aim to expand on the theoretical concepts explored in the presentation. The class will finally be expected to provide feedback to the group on whether they believe the proposed option makes sense and if they think it will work.

At the end of the discussion, a nominated “marking group” will be required to give approximately 3 minutes of constructive feedback to the presenting group based on the marking rubric. This marking group will then award a mark, guided by the marking rubric and filling out the Group Presentation Marking Guide. The marking group’s mark will contribute a maximum of 5 out of 15 for the presentation. The lecturer’s mark will contribute a maximum of 10 out of 15 marks for the presentation. The combined scaled marks will give a total final mark out of 15.

assessment_details.title: Individual Newspaper Article Reviews
assessment_details.task_description:
N e wspaper Article 1 (20%)

By referring to any Australian or International newspapers, find one article that relates to economic concepts discussed in any lecture up to and including that on 26 March 2018. This newspaper article must be written in English, can be scanned or obtained from online sources, and published after 1 January, 2018. Radio and TV transcripts are NOT to be used. Your newspaper article must satisfy these requirements to pass this assessment task.

Using no more than 1000 words , complete the following:

1. Briefly summarise the article in a sentence or two, listing the economic concepts you will need to refer to in this assignment.

2. Support the article’s view by clearly stating any assumptions it makes and applying economic concepts.

3. By using alternative assumptions and economic concepts, critically analyse the article and present a different perspective.

4. Summarise one economic lesson learnt from your analysis. Then, by naming some “other actual company” (in a completely different industry used in the article), discuss how the lesson learnt would be relevant. Then, as a manager of this “other actual company”, explain one recommendation you would implement based on this lesson learnt.

5. Self­ assess your assignment by filling out each section of the Newspaper article Marking Criteria Sheet, being sure to give an overall mark for the assignment. Also complete the section “List specific feedback requests” provided on this Marking Criteria Sheet. Please ensure you honestly self­ assess. This self-­assessment will not influence the grade awarded for this piece of assessment. However, your self-­assessment thoughts are intended to be incorporated into the marker’s feedback. Please ensure your completed self-­assessment marking criteria sheet is attached as the last page of your assignment. The word document Marking Criteria Sheet that you must fill in and attach at the end of your assignment is available on Blackboard.

N o t e : Your first newspaper article assignment may be submitted before Sunday, 1 April 2018 at 11:59 pm. The aim will be to mark and provide early feedback before submitting the second news article (you will get your mark for the first article at this time). Students are encouraged to submit this first article early in order to benefit from the feedback. Otherwise, both news articles may be submitted on the due date shown in the course profile.

N e wspaper Article 2 (20%)

As detailed above for Newspaper Article 1.

I M P O R T A N T : Please ensure that each newspaper article submission is compiled as follows:

1. First page is a cover sheet with your name and student number.

2. Second page is the relevant news article (ensure it is clearly able to be read).

3. Your written work.

4. Final page shows your self-­assessed, including the marks awarded by yourself by filling in the

Marking Criteria sheet provided. You should indicate areas where feedback is required.

If you submit two articles at the same time (ie: the early feedback option is not taken), please ensure that two separate files are submitted, one file for each article, using the relevant Turnitin folders on Blackboard.

Students please note that there is a US version in its 5th edition . You do not want this. The latest Australian edition is here. Lectures do not follow the textbook exactly, but it does provide useful explanations and excersises dealing most of the key topics.

Next 4. Activities

learning_activities.topic: Big picture of economics (Lecture): Establishes the important themes of the course. Outlines learning approaches to be adopted, the structure of the lectures and tutorials and the assessment items. Proceeds on to important questions of economics - what does economics study, why, and how do economists approach these topics of study. Key principles include such things as the assumption of rationality, the hidden moral foundations of utilitarianism, opportunity cost, economic surplus, specialisation, and marginal analysis. Readings/Ref: Hubbard, et al (Chapters 1 and 2); Extra Readings
Tutorial 1 (Tutorial): Workshop session applying key concepts from Lecture 1.

Demand and Supply (Lecture): An introduction to market, focussing on the question What do prices do? Standard approaches to market clearing, including assumed behaviour of supply and demand in the neoclassical model. Of critical importance is the concept of responsiveness or elasticity. Additionally, multiple pricing strategies are discussed, as well as what economists mean by welfare. Readings/Ref: Hubbard, et al (Ch. 3-4 (without Appendix)); Extra Readings
Tutorial 2 (Tutorial): Workshop on Lecture 1 and 2 material Readings/Ref: Extra Readings

Economic efficiency and introduction to production (Lecture): Consumer and producer surpluses, price floors and ceilings, taxes. Introduction to technology, production and costs. Readings/Ref: Barney; Hubbard, et al (Ch. 5-6); Extra Readings
Tutorial 3 (Tutorial): Workshop session on prices, pricing, supply and demand approach to economic analysis. Readings/Ref: Extra Readings

Perfectly competitive equilibrium and Monopoly (Lecture): Firm behaviour in competitive markets in both the short-run and long-run. The notion of 'economic profits', and how perfect competition leads to different forms of efficiency. Introduction to monopoly markets. Readings/Ref: Hubbard, et al (Ch. 7-8 (without Appendix)); Extra Readings
Tutorial 4 (Tutorial): Analysis of case study on monopolies. Readings/Ref: Extra Readings

More alternatives to PC; Porter's 'Five Forces' (Lecture): Analysis and comparison of different forms of price discrimination, monopolistic competition and oligopoly markets to perfect competition. Understanding strategic interactions through game theory. How strategic interactions affect business and economic analysis. Porter's 'Five Forces'. Readings/Ref: Hubbard, et al (Ch. 8(Appendix) and Ch. 9); Extra Readings; Porter
Tutorial 5 (Tutorial): Workshop on monopolistic competition, oligopoly, price discrimination.

Game theory (Lecture): An introduction to game theory and social interactions. Using the concepts to understanding strategic interactions, and particularly how these affect business and economic analysis. Catch up on skipped material from previous weeks. Readings/Ref: CORE (Chapter 4 "Social Interactions")
Tutorial 6 (Tutorial): Workshop on game theory.
Mid-Semester Break (No lecture & Tutorial)

Factors of production and the Government's role (Lecture): Introduction to the labour market, personnel economics and the markets for capital and natural resources. Government intervention: When should it intervene, and how? Discussion of positive and negative externalities. Readings/Ref: Hubbard, et al (Ch. 10-11); Extra Readings

Group presentations 1-2 (Group Work): This tutorial will have the first group presentation. All students are expected to attend presentations each week after class, to learn from the experience, as well as contribute to marking.

Macroeconomics: Basic Concepts (Lecture): Defining and measuring of GDP, unemployment, inflation and interest rates, along with other useful metrics of the macroeconomy. Limitations of these measures and how they affect business are discussed. Readings/Ref: Hubbard, et al (Chapters 12 and 13); Extra Readings
Group presentations 3-4 (Group Work): All students are expected to attend presentations each week after class, to learn from the experience, as well as contribute to marking.

Aggregate expenditure model, and money (Lecture): Introduction to aggregate demand and aggregate expenditure, which a standard was for economists to think about booms and busts. Banking and money creation in the modern economy is also discussed, as many theories show tight link between the mechanisms of money and the business cycle. Readings/Ref: Hubbard, et al (Chapters 14 and 15); Extra Readings
Group presentations 5-6 (Group Work)
Labour Day public holiday (No lecture & Tutorial)

International Economics (Lecture): External accounts, comparative advantage and its (ir)relevance to international trade. Foreign exchange, exchange rates and their impact on business. Policy settings and history of export-led development. Readings/Ref: Hubbard, et al (Chapters 18 and 19); Extra Readings
Group presentations 7-8 (Group Work)
Guest Lecture (Lecture): Guest lecturer to present on a special topic. Readings/Ref: Extra Readings
Group presentations 9-10 (Group Work)

The Behavioural Revolution (Lecture): An introduction to the key concepts of behavioural economics, and how they apply to, support or contradict what we have learned in the course. Readings/Ref: Hubbard, et al (Ch. 3 (Appendix only)); Extra Readings

Short reflection exercise or group presentations (Group Work): Final group presentations (if any). Review of presentations, key lessons from the course, changes to personal behaviour, thinking, final discussions.

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the University's assessment-related procedures can be found on my.UQ. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/services/exams-and-assessment )

Academic Integrity

It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism

The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

An application for an Extension of Assessment Due Date for medical grounds and/or other exceptional circumstances shall be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation by the date specified in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile. Students will be notified of the outcome of their request via the myRequests section of my.UQ (and an email sent to their student account).

Additional details associated with extension requests, including acceptable and unacceptable reasons, may be found at my.UQ .

Feedback on Assessment

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the my.UQ web site. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/academic-progress-and-final-results/querying-result )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

School of Economics
Assessment Guidelines

Definition of Plagiarism

The act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another either intentionally or unintentionally. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

You must make it clear which ideas you have obtained from someone else. Superficial and minor changes do not disguise your use of the words of someone else. You commit plagiarism if you do not acknowledge the source of a direct quote, or a specific piece of writing that you have paraphrased, or even if you describe an idea or concept that you have heard or read somewhere without a reference or acknowledgement.

Examples of plagiarism are:

Direct copying of paragraphs, sentences, a single sentence or significant parts of a sentence;

Direct copying of paragraphs, sentences, a single sentence or significant parts of a sentence with an end reference but without quotation marks around the copied text;

Copying ideas, concepts, research results, computer codes, statistical tables, designs, images, sounds or text or any combination of these;

Paraphrasing, summarising or simply rearranging another person's words, ideas, etc without changing the basic structure and/or meaning of the text;

Referring an idea or interpretation that is not one's own without identifying whose idea or interpretation it is;

A ‘cut and paste' of statements from multiple sources;

Presenting as independent, work done in collaboration with others;

Copying or adapting another student's original work into a submitted assessment item.

Submitting the same work or recycling work in more than one course without prior permission of the course coordinator.

Importance of Referencing

Complete and accurate referencing is essential for academic writing. There are three main reasons why full referencing is essential.

The first is that when another writer’s work is used without being referenced the act of plagiarism has been committed. Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one’s own work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another and is covered by the University’s Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy . 1. The penalty for plagiarism under the Policy can range from zero marks for the assessment item to cancellation of credit for the course.

The second reason for referencing is so that anyone reading the paper can follow up any ideas or concepts that have been presented. This is especially important in academic writing where people involved in research may read the paper. One of the main ways of researching a topic is to read what other people have written and then follow up some of the references they have cited. Then, after those ‘follow-up’ papers have been read the researcher can continue the search-trail by tracking down more references cited in those follow-up papers, and so on. This is known as a bibliographical chain. This research is difficult if not impossible to complete if papers are not fully and accurately referenced.

Thirdly, referencing is important as it adds credibility to the argument that you are presenting. If an argument is to have any credibility it must be supported by evidence and that evidence must be referenced.

If a reader of your paper can read something that you have written and ask questions like; who says? Or How do you know that? Or On what do you base that comment? Then what you have written needs to be referenced. For more information on the importance of, and direction on referencing please refer to the School of Economics Assignment Presentation Guide .

Consequences of Plagiarism

Students should be aware that the School of Economics does not hesitate to use the procedures available under the University of Queensland Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy to investigate suspected misconduct involving plagiarism.

The penalties imposed for cases where a finding of misconduct is made vary, based on the seriousness of the case and range across but are not limited to:

a reduction in the mark for the assessment piece or cancellation of the mark for the assessment piece;

a requirement to complete further or repeat work for the subject;

the cancellation of credit for the subject;

expulsion from the University.

Students should also be aware of the fact that when a finding of misconduct is made that this is recorded on the University’s central misconduct database.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses

Students on a placement course – also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice – should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-placement-courses ) for detailed information.

Working with Children

Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/1.60.07-working-children ) to find out how to apply for a ‘blue card’.

Students with a Disability

Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety

Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Fitness to Practise

Students enrolled in programs and courses that include a practical placement component (also known as work integrated learning, clinical immersion, clinical placement, clinical practice, externship, fieldwork, industry experience, industry study, internship, practicum, teaching practice, work placement) should refer to the University policy Fitness to Practise ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.30.14-fitness-practise ) for detailed information on issues of competence and on management of concerns relating to patient and public safety.

Next Summary
course_introduction:
This course covers a range of economic theories and concepts of direct relevance and application to the business environment. The course assumes little or no formal training in economics, notwithstanding the practical economic problems that businesses and consumers confront daily. It aims to provide a solid grounding in the way economists think, the core theories of contempory economics and how this is relates to the business environment and business strategy. Emphasis is placed upon the application of economic theory rather than the theory itself in a manner reminiscent of the business economists of the USA who form an integral part of American corporate strategy.. This theme is developed not only in lectures, but also through student activities including reviewing current newspaper articles, in-class case presentations, and writing a reflective essay on their current work organization and experience. The course includes both micro economics (a study of the economics of consumers, firms, individual industries and Government agencies) and macroeconomics (the study of the domestic and international economies as whole and the environment that firms and other economic agents face externally and over which they exercise little control). The early lectures begin microeconomic concepts with an emphasis on market (demand and supply) conditions, price setting, profit and revenue and cost considerations and differing market structures. Later, problems in allocating available resources with limited information and knowledge are discussed. The linkages between economic concepts and business disciplines such as marketing, finance, and human resource management will become apparent through the course. The lectures in the latter part of the course stress the macro element and are designed to examine the way in which the wider economic environment within Australia and overseas influences the micro decisions of economic agents. Concepts such as Government policy (including taxation and monetary policy) shifts in exchange rates, Gross Domestic Product (GDP) and Gross National Expenditure (GNE), inflation, unemployment, aggregate demand and supply, and international trade will be analysed. Introductions and foundations of game theory and behavioural economics, as they apply to business and strategy, will also be discussion. At the end of the course the student will be able to and critically evaluate articles in (for example) the Australian Financial Review and The Economist and determine their relevance to his or her organisation.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

Dr David Smerdon offers this course for the first time so feedback is currently not available. We look forward to receiving your responses at the end of the semester.

attributes.attribute: A2 . A broad understanding of the field of study, including how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.attribute: A3 . A comprehensive and in-depth knowledge in the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A7 . An appreciation of the link between theory and practice .
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .
B5 . The ability to practise as part of an interdisciplinary team .
C2 . The ability to work and learn independently and effectively .
C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C5 . The ability to formulate and investigate problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .
C6 . The abilities and skills that provide a foundation for future leadership roles.
D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D4 . The ability to process material and to critically analyse and integrate information from a wide range of sources.
D5 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions using an evidence-based approach .
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E3 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .
E7 . The ability to work effectively and sensitively across all areas of society .
E8 . An understanding of and respect for the roles and expertise of associated disciplines .

course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

0-19%

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

20-44.99%

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

A common cause of students getting this grade on an answer, aside from having done little work or achieved a poor understanding, is a failure to answer the question that was set (for example, if the question says 'explore the relationships between the following concepts', you must explore the relationships between them, not define or describe them), or a failure to attempt all parts of the question. So, read the wording very carefully. 45-49.99%

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

A basic requirement of being able to think like an economist is demonstrating that you consider the relative costs and benefits of alternative possible choices and the implications of differences in the incentives they offer to decision makers. If you fail to do this, you may have a hard time getting a 4, let alone a better grade, even if you look at the pros and cons of a particular choice from the standpoint of relevant economic theory. 50-64.99%

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Note here the phrase 'ability to apply': if your answers primarily report on facts that you have gathered or merely summarize the work of others you will be failing to show what you can do with economic concepts as tools of analysis and you will not score more than a 4. For a 5 of beyond, you must show that you can think like an economist rather than merely displaying abilities in terms of researching facts or what some economists think. 65-74.99%

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

To get beyond a 5, it is helpful to be able to demonstrate that you know what is predicted by relevant economic theory in the context in question and consider how well the predictions match up with facts. 75-84.99%

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

Integrative thinking that relates concepts from different parts of the course will be highly regarded as will the application of ideas/arguments from reading beyond the prescribed text or lecture notes. 85-100%
late_submission:
Requests for the granting of extensions must be made online via https://my.uq.edu.au/node/218/2#2 with supporting documentation at least 24 hours prior to the submission date. If an extension is approved, the new agreed date for submission will be noted on the application and the student notified through their student email. Extensions cannot exceed the number of days you suffered from a medical condition, as stated on the medical certificate.

Students who are ill for more than 14 days may wish to apply for withdrawal without academic penalty. The guidelines for late submission of progressive assessment may be accessed through: http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/extension-progressive-assessment .

The penalty for late submission of an assessment item, without prior approval for an extension, will be 5% of the total mark available for the assessment item for each calendar day after the due date .
other_assessment_information:
The School of Economics is committed to reducing the incidence of plagiarism. Further information on plagiarism and how to avoid an allegation of plagiarism is available under Section 6. Policies and Guidelines - 6.1. Assessment Related Policies & Guidelines of this Course Profile.

ASSIGNMENT SUBMISSION

Unless otherwise specified, ALL assignments must be lodged by the due date and time stated in the course profile. For this course, students are required to submit assignments via Blackboard/Turnitin.
